news_communities.title: Malden woman accused of threatening federal agents making immigration arrest
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_communities.summary:
A Massachusetts woman is accused of threatening to kill federal agents who were conducting immigration enforcement in Malden.

Bethany Abigail Terrill, 37, was charged with threatening a United States official, the U.S. Attorney’s Office said Thursday.

Prosecutors say that Terrill approached the agents as they were making an arrest outside Malden District Court on Sept. 29, describing her as verbally abusive. She allegedly shouted things like “ICE is here, ICE is here,” “You guys are monsters, this is insane,” “Sir, what’s your name, what’s your name,” “I can try to help you,” and “I am an American civilian, I have a right to be here,” and called the agents Nazis.

She is further accused of trying to push past the agents, yelling “Charlie Kirk died, and we love it… We’re coming for you, gonna kill you.” 

According to the statement of probable cause, an agent heard the threat and proceeded to detain Terrill, who they said was “actively resistant and fought to escape.”

Officials say the agents were wearing body cameras, and Terrill was also filming at the time. In the statement of probable cause, investigators say the threat can be heard in the footage from Terrill’s phone.

The agents on scene were from multiple agencies, including Homeland Security Investigations (HSI), ICE and the FBI.

During her appearance in U.S. District Court in Boston on Thursday, a public defender said Terrill owns a salon and was struggling with legal issues in an unrelated case.

Judge Paul Levenson ordered Terrill be released with conditions, saying “federal agents have a right to not be subjected to threats.”

Terrill was ordered as part of her conditions of release to participate in a mental health program for treatment on a regular basis, to develop a safety plan for her own safety and others, avoid contact with potential witnesses, have no contact with any federal official, family or otherwise in any way or form, refrain from alcohol and the use of any illegal drugs, to avoid contact with law enforcement, and to refrain from making any further threats.

She didn’t speak to reporters outside of court.

The charge of threatening a U.S. official carries a sentence of up to 10 years in prison, three years of supervised release and a fine of $250,000.

news_communities.title: Cities bordering Everett unite in Revolution soccer stadium transparency push
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
Four cities that border Everett, Massachusetts, have banded together in a push for more involvement in the New England Revolution’s plans for a soccer-specific stadium in the city.

The mayors of Revere, Medford, Malden and Chelsea formed a coalition called City Mayors for Citizens that they said will seek transparency on plans for the stadium, including the impacts it will have on their communities.

The 25,000-seat stadium proposal has progressed in the state Legislature but still faces plenty of hurdles. The City Mayors for Citizens said they are worried about how the building on along the Mystic River might impact local infrastructure, public transit and quality of life.

“A project of this size and scale will have significant regional impacts,” said Revere Mayor Patrick Keefe Jr said in a statement. “We want to ensure that a stadium is contributing positively to the communities it is drawing resources from. Our residents should not bear the burdens of a private stadium that has strong potential to negatively affect their day-to-day lives.”

Asked for a response to the statement, a Revolution club spokesperson noted that it expects to work with neighboring communities after it reaches agreements with Everett and Boston, which would allow the property to have its port designation removed and the process of construction to continue.

The spokesperson also noted that the team thanked the mayors and promised to review the concerns raised in their letter, sent Monday.

news_communities.title: Video shows Salvadoran man being detained by ICE in Malden
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain, Marc Fortier and Mark Garfinkel
news_communities.summary:
The wife of a Salvadoran man says he was violently taken into custody by immigration officials on his way to work in Malden, Massachusetts, on Wednesday morning.

The incident occurred shortly before 6 a.m. in the area of 28 Kennedy Drive.

Leslie Perlera Gonzalez said she received a distressing call from her husband, Edgar Hernan Elias Escobar, shortly before 6 a.m. as he was heading to his construction job. She rushed to Kennedy Drive, where she said Elias Escobar was surrounded by immigration officers.

“I heard them smash his window, and he was saying, ‘Ow, you’re hurting me,'” Perlera Gonzalez said. “When I arrived on the scene, he had a Taser on his face.”

The 31-year-old had no criminal record and was in the process of adjusting his immigration status after the couple married last year, noted Perlera Gonzalez.

“He’s never come close to doing anything that would be considered a crime,” she said.

She alleged that agents used an unmarked car to ram her husband’s vehicle, forcing him off the road before detaining him.

Malden police said Elias Escobar sustained minor injuries. He was transported on a stretcher to a hospital in Melrose, where Perlera Gonzalez said she was not allowed to see him.

About an hour later, he was moved to a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement processing facility in Burlington.

A spokesperson from the Department of Homeland Security said in a statement that Elias Escobar was the target of the ICE operation and suffered “no injuries.”

“On September 17, 2025, ICE conducted an immigration enforcement operation in Malden, Massachusetts to arrest Edgar Hernan Elias-Escobar, an illegal alien from El Salvador. During the arrest, the illegal alien refused to comply with officer commands to rolldown his window. The officers took appropriate action and followed their training to use the minimum amount of force necessary to resolve the situation in a manner that ensured the success of the operation and prioritized the safety of the public and our officers,” the spokesperson said in a statement. “Out of an abundance of caution, ICE law enforcement escorted Elias-Escobar to the hospital for evaluations. Medical staff found he had no injuries and discharged him. He was then escorted to the Boston Filed office for processing and will be placed in removal proceedings.”

“I’ve been expecting this from somebody I know every day since Donald Trump was elected,” said Amiee Parco, an attorney with Greater Boston Legal Services and a colleague of Perlera Gonzalez. “I’ve been waiting for it to affect me personally.”

Friends and coworkers gathered outside the Burlington facility, joining a weekly protest to support Elias Escobar’s release and that of other detainees.

“He’s a really wonderful person,” said Alyssa Rao, a friend of the couple. “I think this is going to impact everyone.”

After Wednesday’s ordeal, Perlera Gonzalez fears what her husband is going through while detained.

“I’m terrified of what could be happening when no one is watching,” she said.

At last check, Escobar was still at the facility. It’s unclear how long he’ll be held — often, people stay several days, though the facility is meant to detain people for hours.

His lawyer is trying to prevent the government from sending him to a far-away detention center, and from deportation.

ICE has ramped up arrests across greater Boston in recent weeks under “Operation Patriot 2.0,” detaining immigrants in public spaces.

Gov. Maura Healey spoke out against the sweeps on Tuesday, saying federal immigration enforcement officers are “creating an incredible amount of fear and intimidation.”

“Some of it seems purposeful, to be honest. Why else are you parked outside a church? Why are you parked outside a community center?”

The governor said her office has received no information from ICE about who is being detained or why.

“This is not a furtherance of public safety,” she said. “In fact, it undermines public safety.”

Asked what she can do to protest White House policy, Healey said: “I join with other governors in urging the Trump administration, in the strongest possible terms, to change course.”

State House News Service contributed to this report.

news_boston.title: Stoughton police chief says town is trying to force her out
news_boston.author: Amelia Fabiano and Asher Klein
news_boston.summary:
A dispute is unfolding between the town of Stoughton, Massachusetts, and its police chief.

An acting chief is in place, the town confirmed Wednesday, but it didn’t say why. Donna McNamara, who’s been the chief for almost a decade, said Stoughton is trying to force her out of her job after filing a complaint against the town manager.

McNamara’s attorney told NBC10 Boston Wednesday that the chief never stepped down or told she was officially out in the first place.

“She is not on leave. She hasn’t resigned. She’s still under her contract. And, in fact, she learned today with everybody else that she’s been temporarily replaced,” attorney Leah Barrault said.

The town confirmed that Deputy Chief James O’Connor is serving as acting chief, but said in a statement that it doesn’t discuss personnel matters.

NBC10 Boston has reached out to the Stoughton Select Board and police department but haven’t gotten a reply.

Barrault said that McNamara was told earlier this month that she needed to resign or be fired, and that she refused to resign. She also filed a harassment complaint against Town Manager Thomas Calter earlier this week, alleging he’d been gossiping and disparaging her while calling and texting her every day for two weeks, according to the lawyer.

It was McNamara who revealed in 2022 that three former Stoughton police officers had inappropriate sexual relationships with a young woman who investigators had determined died by suicide after becoming pregnant.

If you or someone you know is in crisis, call or text 988 to reach the Suicide and Crisis Lifeline or chat live at 988lifeline.org. You can also visit SpeakingOfSuicide.com/resources for additional support.

McNamara told reporters at a news conference that the information about what happened to Sandra Birchmore was “deeply troubling as a human being and a police chief.” Birchmore had met the three officers as a teenager through the department’s youth explorers program.

All resigned before they were interviewed as part of the investigation, McNamara said, noting that Birchmore “was failed by, manipulated by and used by people of authority that she admired and trusted right until her final days.”

Years later, after Birchmore’s family investigated and disputed the state medical examiner’s finding that the woman died by suicide, one of the officers was arrested on federal charges — the murder trial is set to go ahead later this year.

When the officer was indicted, McNamara released a statement calling the allegations against her former officer “the single worst act of not just professional misconduct but indeed human indecency that I have observed in a nearly three-decade career in law enforcement.” She added that she’d been working to ensure that Birchmore received justice, since the woman “received no justice during her life.”

news_boston.title: ‘Next Match on Us' as New England Revolution look to build on World Cup hype
news_boston.author: Oscar Margain
news_boston.summary:
The New England Revolution hope the excitement surrounding the FIFA World Cup will translate into long-term support for Major League Soccer.

The club launched a promotion called “Next Match on Us,” offering two free tickets to Saturday’s match against Atlanta United to people who have never attended a Revolution game. Team officials said the goal is not simply to fill seats but to introduce new fans to the club and keep the momentum generated by the World Cup alive.

“We really worked hard during the World Cup to start to build that momentum to the restart of the season,” said Brian Bilello, president of the New England Revolution.

Bilello said the tournament reinforced how popular soccer has become in the United States.

“I think what people don’t realize now is how many people in this country like soccer,” he said.

He believes the World Cup demonstrated that top-level soccer can appeal to a much broader audience than longtime fans.

“The World Cup really showed when you’ve got great soccer, you’ve got a great event, that sports fans — not just soccer fans, but sports fans of this country — will really engage at incredibly high levels,” Bilello said.

The club views the promotion as more than a giveaway.

“It’s not really just a free ticket to the game, but it’s really your introduction to becoming a fan of the club,” Bilello said. “There’s a real special experience and connection you have with your club when you come to a game and you feel the energy in the building, not just of the players on the pitch, but the other fans and how they support and engage with the players during the match.”

The Revolution are also counting on the return of U.S. national team goalkeeper Matt Turner to help attract fans.

“Off the back of the excitement of the World Cup in the area, you know, continue to make new fans and new memories for people that want to support this great team,” Turner said.

Fans welcomed the promotion.

“I think it’s a great, great idea to bring people for free in the stadium and to help make the league greater and bigger,” soccer fan Julian Lutz said.

While Seojoune Park who has never been to a Revs match said, “I’m a big fan of soccer, so maybe if there’s a free ticket, I will go to see it and watch the match.”

Kayla Osegueda has also yet to catch a Revs match. She believes the timing for the promotion is right.

“After all the hype of the World Cup and stuff like that, I think people are eager to get more soccer experiences and stuff, and I think this is a great way to do that,” she said.

The Revolution expect more than 10,000 first-time fans to attend Saturday’s match against Atlanta. Fans who register through the promotion will also receive free swag and be treated to a postgame fireworks show. Registration closes Thursday.

news_boston.title: What to know about the Charles River's potentially harmful algae bloom
news_boston.author: Brianna Borghi
news_boston.summary:
Massachusetts health officials are warning people and pets to avoid part of the Charles River while they look into a potentially harmful cyanobacteria bloom.

The Massachusetts Department of Public Health on Tuesday issued an advisory for the river between Kendrick Street in Needham and Spring Street in West Roxbury. The affected area includes Cutler Park and Millennium Park.

Warning signs are now posted at access points along the river.

“I just noticed them and people have been talking about them where I live,” dog owner Brian Drayton said.

The advisory is changing how some dog owners use the area.

“He was just trying to get near the water and last time we went for a walk with him, we actually allowed him to drink some of the water. So now we pulled him away from the water and tried to keep him safe,” dog owner Angie Almonte said.

The warning comes after a dog died from liver failure following a possible exposure to algal toxins near Millennium Park. State health officials have not confirmed that cyanobacteria caused the dog’s illness or death.

The type of algae suspected in the area may be difficult to spot, said Marielena Lima, river science program manager for the Charles River Watershed Association.

“I’ve heard anecdotes from the dog owner saying, you know, the water looked clear, and that is possible with benthic cyanobacteria blooms,” Lima said.

Benthic cyanobacteria grow along the bottom of a body of water. Lima said that is different from the blooms people commonly see on the Charles River.

“Normally the kinds of cyanobacteria blooms we see in the Charles are more in the water column, so they’re towards the surface of the water and that’s why you see a green haze or scum on the top of the water surface,” she said.

Warm temperatures, sunlight, excess nutrient runoff after rainfall and stagnant or slow-moving water can promote cyanobacteria growth, according to the Department of Public Health.

“The way the temperatures have risen, we’re gonna get more and more of this so it’s something you sort of expect after a while,” Drayton said.

Some cyanobacteria can also produce toxins that may make people and animals sick. Exposure can cause skin and eye irritation, respiratory problems, vomiting and diarrhea. More serious cases can affect the liver, kidneys or nervous system.

People should avoid swimming, kayaking or coming into contact with the water or shoreline in the affected area. Pet owners should not allow animals to swim in or drink from the river.

Anyone who comes into contact with the water or algae should rinse off immediately. Pet owners should call a veterinarian if an animal develops symptoms such as vomiting, staggering, drooling or convulsions.

State health officials collected water samples Wednesday to test for cyanobacteria cells and toxins. Laboratory results are expected Thursday, and officials will issue updated recommendations after reviewing them.

Shvartsman was last seen around 3 a.m. in the area of East Newton Street.

Police did not give a physical description of Shvartsman, but shared a photo.

Family told police Shvartsman may have taken a pair of brown slippers with him.

Anyone with information is asked to call 617-343-4250. Anonymous tips can also be left by calling 1-800-494-8477, by texting “TIP” to 27463, or by visiting this website.

The Boston Police Department’s fugitive unit arrested 23-year-old Justin Ferrer of Dorchester Wednesday. He was taken into custody on the 1900 block of Dorchester Avenue.

A straight warrant from Dorchester District Court charges Ferrer with murder, police said.

news_bc.title: BC To Launch New Krantz Institute for AI, Ethics, and Humanity
news_bc.author: Danica Bergen
news_bc.summary:
Boston College will launch the Krantz Institute for Artificial Intelligence, Ethics, and Humanity to leverage the University’s Jesuit teaching in the study of the technology, it announced earlier this month.

The institute is made possible by a gift from University Trustee Jason Krantz, BC ’95, and Keely Krantz, BC ’95. 

“The gift will establish the Krantz Institute for Artificial Intelligence, Ethics, and Humanity, which will utilize BC faculty, visiting scholars, and industry partners to examine AI’s long-term opportunities and impact from a humanistic perspective, while instilling BC students with the judgement, wisdom, and critical thinking necessary to help them become ethical leaders in its deployment,” the announcement read.

The University did not disclose the size of the couple’s contribution, but called it a “transformative gift.”

Jason is the executive chairman and founder of Definitive Healthcare, a publicly traded data and analytics company. He is also the managing partner and founder of Breachway Capital, a venture firm focused on B2B software, data, and AI innovation. 

“Given Boston College’s commitment to formative education and the Jesuit principles of ethical discernment, BC, in our mind, is uniquely positioned to lead the conversation about the consequences of AI, what the technology means for humanity, and how it might be best leveraged in service to others,” he said in the announcement.

Keely is the president and founder of the O’Dell Women’s Center, a nonprofit organization that provides low-income women in Western Massachusetts with access to career and education opportunities. 

“We want to see values and ethics as foundational components in how the world approaches AI and AI education,” Keely said. “Additionally, we want BC students to use their formative education and discernment skills to help lead and improve the application of AI in the future.” 

BC had already begun investing resources into the study of AI before the Krantzes’ donation. According to the announcement, the University has over 30 faculty members teaching courses concerning AI, and 11 faculty have received external AI-research grants. 

The Krantz Institute hopes to build upon the existing investment by creating internal opportunities and promoting global learning. In addition to offering internships, seed grants, and AI programs to BC students, the institute will also gather pioneering scholars for future conferences and symposia on AI and ethics. 

“Boston College and Boston in general should be the gathering place for discussions where leaders across different fields, including computer scientists, business executives, government leaders, theologians, and ethicists, come together with faculty and students to discuss the impact of this critical technology on the future of humanity,” said Jason. 

Keely added that the institute aims to place the University at the center of human-centered AI innovation. 

“The Krantz Institute aspires to be different from anything we have seen in higher education and will help position Boston College to have a global impact on the future of AI,” said Keely. 

All of BC’s nine schools will be involved in the new institute. 

According to Robert L. and Judith T. Winston Provost and Dean of Faculties David Quigley, BC is aiming to hire an executive director for the Krantz Institute in the 2026–2027 academic year.

In the interim, the institute will be led by Morrissey College of Arts and Sciences Dean Gregory Kalscheur, S.J., and Carroll School of Management John and Linda Powers Family Dean Andrew Boynton.

Kalscheur and Boynton emphasized the potential far-reaching impact of the new institute, which aims to put Catholic, Jesuit thought at the center of AI learning. 

“This investment makes more widely available our faculty’s shared work of inquiry across the disciplines in service of a more just and human future, and enhances our students’ education, helping to form women and men who are not only highly skilled, but also possess sound judgment, careful reflection, and discerning wisdom,” said Kalscheur. 

Boynton noted that the Krantzes’ generosity reflects the University’s fundamental mission. 

“When you combine our enduring motto ‘Ever to Excel’ with the generosity of alumni like Jason and Keely Krantz, Boston College becomes an unstoppable force,” said Boynton. 

The post BC To Launch New Krantz Institute for AI, Ethics, and Humanity appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: 90 St. Thomas More To Become Martignetti Hall After Trustee Gift
news_bc.author: Nikita Osadchiy
news_bc.summary:
Boston College will rename 90 St. Thomas More—a sophomore residential hall located on Lower Campus—Martignetti Hall, following a donation from University alumni Beth V. Martignetti, BC ’76, and Trustee Associate Carmine A. Martignetti, BC ’76, the University announced Tuesday.

“The gift, which comes as the couple celebrates 50 years since their BC graduation, will provide unrestricted support to Boston College, allowing the University and its leadership to address its most urgent needs,” a University announcement said. “In grateful recognition of this gift, Boston College will name the residence hall located at 90 Thomas More Drive Martignetti Hall.”

The University did not disclose the size of the couple’s contribution or specify how it would be earmarked.

In the announcement, the couple thanked outgoing University President Rev. William P. Leahy, S.J., for his contributions, and expressed their enthusiasm for the continuity that his successor, Rev. Jack Butler, S.J., will bring when he steps into the role next month.

“We have so much respect for Fr. Leahy and all that he has done for Boston College, which is now among the best Universities in the country thanks to his leadership,” Beth said in the announcement. “Our appreciation doesn’t end there—we’re so delighted for the transition to Fr. Jack Butler and the continuity that he will bring.”

Leahy is set to resign on July 31 following his historic 30-year tenure.

The now-Martignetti Hall was constructed in 1993, alongside its sister residential dorm, Vanderslice Hall. The building contains 60 residential suites and can house up to 380 students, according to the University website. The hall has maintained its residential address as its title until the donation.

Carmine has been a member of the University’s board of trustees since 2015 and is the president of family-owned Martignetti Companies, a wine, spirits, and beer distributor in New England. He and his wife are residents of Chestnut Hill.

Carmine previously served as the chairman of the Tabor Academy Board of Trustees—a college preparatory boarding and day school in Marion, Mass.—from 2014 to 2021, while Beth worked as a surgical nurse at Brigham and Women’s Hospital following graduation from the University.

The Martignettis did not immediately respond to a request for comment made through their company.

The post 90 St. Thomas More To Become Martignetti Hall After Trustee Gift appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: Reimagining Public Media: NPR’s President Talks Its Current State and Its Future
news_bc.author: Mac Cobb
news_bc.summary:
Over the past year, actions taken by President Donald Trump’s administration have framed public media’s role as a divisive and partisan issue. 

But Katherine Maher sees public media as something that should look to bridge social or political divides.

“No one puts a CNN bumper sticker on their car,” Maher said. “There is a deep, emotional relationship that people have with the idea of being someone who participates in the public media.”

Maher, who has served as the president and CEO of National Public Radio (NPR) since 2024, sat down with The Atlantic’s Evan Smith on May 29 as part of The WBUR Festival in Boston.

Before NPR, Maher was the CEO of the Wikimedia Foundation for five years and served as an adviser to the U.S. Secretary of State’s Foreign Affairs Policy Board from 2022 to 2024. 

Maher began her talk, which was moderated by Smith, by discussing the May 2025 defunding of public media by Trump and Congress, which ultimately cut $1.1 billion worth of funding to the Corporation for Public Broadcasting, $120 million of which went to public radio.

“When federal funding was taken off the table by Congress in their decision last year, we knew that there would be a series of consequences,” Maher said.

Those consequences have manifested in recent weeks, as NPR has had to reduce its workforce by 36 people through layoffs, buyouts, or by leaving empty positions unfilled. But Maher is confident that those cuts will end in the near future. 

“You have to do [these cuts] in a way that prevents you from having to go back to that well,” Maher said. “Our goal was to do that. We believe we’ve accomplished that.” 

But with the American public’s trust in media at an all-time low—just 28 percent, according to a recent Gallup poll—Maher sees NPR’s preservation of local media as an important way to keep Americans trusting the news.

“Eighty-five percent of Americans think that local public, not just public media, but local media, is trustworthy and adds value to their community,” Maher said. “The presence of local media is an indicator of lower rates of polarization, greater rates of trust in democratic institutions, but also in your neighbors.” 

With this trust in mind, Maher discussed how people’s decline in radio consumption and changing work habits have forced NPR to adapt its news distribution. 

“We are moving to a model that really encourages digital engagement,” Maher said. “We need to have a digital relationship, because that is where we are today.”

She went on to acknowledge that this movement will force NPR to overcome some of the issues it has had in the past with online coverage.

“There’s a way in the past that the network competed against itself when it came to digital,” she said. “Now my view is that we have to bring everybody together into a singular platform and place where we’re able to say that, you know, all these folks may be consuming information or engaging with everything from Tiny Desk, Morning Edition to your local public affairs show, but they’re doing it in a home that feels like public radio.”

According to Maher, the goal of public media—specifically public radio—is simple: to reduce polarization and get people to recognize each other as people.

“Audio requires you to hear someone—it requires you to listen,” Maher said. “And when we do our work best, we are asking you to listen to someone who you might fundamentally disagree with and also you cannot deny their humanity. That is what public radio can do.”

The post Reimagining Public Media: NPR’s President Talks Its Current State and Its Future appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: BC’s Historic Season Ends With 8–3 Loss to Liberty
news_bc.author: Maria Stefanoudakis
news_bc.summary:
A historic season came to an end on Sunday afternoon as Boston College baseball fell to Liberty for the second time in three days, knocking the Eagles out of the Athens Regional with an 8–3 loss. 

The Eagles, who are coming off the greatest season in program history after winning 17 ACC games and 37 overall, went up 2–0 early in Sunday’s win-or-go-home matchup. But a five-run third inning from Liberty (43–20, 21–9 CUSA) proved too much for BC (37–23, 17–13 ACC) to overcome, and the Eagles exited the game with a trip back to Chestnut Hill looming. 

Ty Mainolfi smacked a home run to right center on just the second at-bat of the game, giving BC an early lead. Brady Miller got off to an efficient start, too, pitching a 1-2-3 first inning. Luke Gallo hit a leadoff double in the bottom of the second, and Gunnar Johnson drove him home with an RBI single. It seemed like the Eagles were rolling. 

Only, that didn’t last. 

Miller retired three batters in order in the bottom of the second, including striking out two. But things started to go south in the third, as a pop up from Jack Toomey stranded Nick Wang on second. The bottom of the frame was when things spiraled out of control. 

A throwing error allowed Liberty to get a runner on second before the Flames had registered a hit. A hit-by-pitch, walk, and a two-RBI single later, Liberty had tied the game. Another BC error—this time in the form of a failed pickoff attempt by Miller—allowed another runner to get home. An RBI triple from Jordan Jaffe and an RBI double from Nick Barone were the icing on the cake. Just like that, Liberty had stolen a 5–2 lead. 

The Flames added on another run in the fourth thanks to a home run from Josh Campos. The Eagles, meanwhile, were unable to generate any offense. Gallo managed a single in the sixth, but that was the Eagles’ only hit after the second inning. 

Liberty scored three more runs across the sixth and seventh innings after Sean Hard relieved Miller. Down 8–2 in the bottom of the seventh, John Kwiatkowski came in for Hard and forced two quick outs to send the game into the eighth. 

Wang and Kyle Wolff both drew walks in the eighth, but a Johnson strikeout ended the frame. Though sophomore transfer John Mitchell was able to silence the Flames in the bottom of the eighth and the Eagles generated one run in the ninth, a Wang flyout ultimately ended BC’s time in Athens—and its season.

The post BC’s Historic Season Ends With 8–3 Loss to Liberty appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: Mudd Leads Eagles to 8–4 Win Over LIU as BC Keeps Playoff Run Alive
news_bc.author: Maria Stefanoudakis
news_bc.summary:
There’s always a moment of silence right before the first pitch. Everyone in the ballpark seems to hold their breath briefly, eagerly awaiting the result as the ball whistles through the air for the first time. 

The silence that came before Tyler Mudd’s first pitch in the Athens Regional game between Boston College baseball and Long Island ended in cheers from the BC dugout. The ball flew perfectly into the strike zone—in retrospect, that moment was a sign of what was to come. 

Mudd pitched 7.1 innings, dealing 10 strikeouts and no walks while giving up seven hits and four runs. Four of those hits and three of those runs came in the eighth inning, when Long Island (30–22, 26–7 Northeast) staged a small rally and cut the Eagles’ (37–22, 17–13 Atlantic Coast) lead to four. But BC pulled through and captured an 8–4 win, keeping their postseason hopes alive following a loss to Liberty on Friday. 

“I know we had gotten the offensive [going] early, but I thought the story of the game was Tyler [Mudd] going as far as he did,” BC head coach Todd Interdonato said. “It was just nice for us to get our style of play on the field.”

After scoring only three runs—all off solo shots—in Friday’s loss, the Eagles found a more balanced offensive approach in Saturday’s noon matchup on Foley Field. 

“I think that’s kind of what we’ve been preaching, especially the past two or three weeks—obviously, things haven’t gone great offensively, but we just kept on preaching about finding our rhythm within our offense, because that’s what we’ve done all year,” BC’s Carter Hendrickson said. 

Julio Solier, the man of the hour after his two home runs on Friday, remained hot, leading off with a line drive double that bounced its way through the gaps in the center field defense with precision. A Ty Mainolfi sacrifice groundout advanced Solier to third, and an RBI single from Nick Wang drove home the Eagles’ first run of the day in the top of the first inning. 

Mudd retired the first seven batters he faced before finally giving up a single in the bottom of the third. But when asked about his performance, the graduate pitcher out of Shrewsberry, Mass. was eager to credit his teammates. 

“The defense behind me played unbelievable. Luke [Gallo] made that diving play early. I don’t think that stats really show, [LIU] hit some balls hard,” Mudd said. “[My teammates] just played unbelievable defensively and got up to an early lead, so it made my job very easy.”

Meanwhile, BC’s offense extended its lead, starting with an RBI sacrifice bunt from Ben Williams and a two-RBI home run from Hendrickson in the second inning. 

“I feel like we were creating a lot of momentum today,” Hendrickson said. “Especially in pregame, a lot of our talk was, ‘Okay, once we find our rhythm, it’s going to be hard to stop us.’ I feel like today we really stacked a lot of at-bats on top of each other, and that’s why we’ve had so much success this season. I think we kind of found our rhythm back today.” 

The Eagles tacked on three more runs in the top of the fourth, starting with Solier hit by a pitch that brought Kyle Wolff home. In the next at-bat, Mainolfi’s bat struck gold for a two-RBI single. BC’s final run of the day came in the fifth, when Jack Toomey hit his second home run in as many games to make it 8–0 Eagles.

After dealing five strikeouts, Mudd finally gave up a run in the bottom of the fifth when Joseph Durso hit a solo shot over the wall in left field. Neither team scored again until the eighth—but when Long Island’s bats finally started finding the ball, BC faltered. The dam broke when Ryan Rivera hammered a three-run home run, cutting the Eagles’ lead to 8–4 and prompting Interdonato to end Mudd’s day at 7.1 innings. 

Chase Hartsell headed to the mound, but gave up a walk on the first at-bat he faced and was immediately replaced by Kyle Kipp. Kipp struck out two batters looking, ending the frame and hindering Long Island’s chances at a comeback. BC’s offense was silent in the ninth, but its defense pulled through once again in the bottom of the frame, holding the lead to come away with an 8–4 win.

The post Mudd Leads Eagles to 8–4 Win Over LIU as BC Keeps Playoff Run Alive appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: BC Falls to Liberty 4–3 After Ninth-Inning Collapse in Opening Game of NCAA Tournament
news_bc.author: Maria Stefanoudakis
news_bc.summary:
Boston College baseball isn’t known as a hard-hitting team—far from it, in fact. The Eagles hit the fewest home runs in the ACC this season (40), 32 fewer than the second-lowest team. 

But the iron-clad identity the Eagles have sported all season, often defined by small ball and a distinct ability to run, came into question on Friday afternoon in Athens, Ga. BC scored all three of its runs off solo shots, including two from Julio Solier and one from Jack Toomey. 

The hard-hitting magic wasn’t enough for the Eagles (36–22, 17–13 Atlantic Coast) to knock off Liberty, however. The Flames (42–19, 21–9 Conference-USA) took a 4–3 win in the opening game of the Athens Regional thanks to two ninth-inning runs, as BC lost the first NCAA Tournament game it has played in since 2023. 

“I thought the difference when I look at it, I could be wrong, I don’t think we got the leadoff guy on until the ninth,” BC head coach Todd Interdonato said. 

After hitting back-to-back RBI singles in the first inning, the Flames maintained a 2–0 lead through the top of the fifth, as starting pitcher Ben Blair dealt seven strikeouts over 7.0 innings pitched. The bottom of the fifth didn’t start much differently for the Eagles, as Carter Hendrickson grounded out to the shortstop on a loaded count. Then, Solier stepped up to the plate. 

Despite leading the Eagles in hits (80), Solier hit only one home run all season. On Friday, though, he caught fire. His first homer of the day flew over the wall in center field, cutting Liberty’s lead to 2–1. He did it again in the bottom of the seventh, sending the ball soaring just over the Georgia logo on the outfield wall to tie the game 2–2. 

“I just like winning. That’s all that matters to me,” Solier said. “I put my praise to God and hit those home runs for the team, not only for me. I play for them. I don’t really play for me.”

While the Eagles—except for Solier, that is—struggled offensively, BC’s defense kept the game in reach. Starting senior A.J. Colarusso recovered after Liberty’s two-run opening frame, and did not allow another score in his 7.0 innings pitched. 

“I thought A.J. in the seventh was electric getting out of there,” Interdonato said. “We were having a conversation about letting him go back out.” 

But Gavin Soares entered in the top of the eighth, and struck out three in another fruitless frame for the Flames. 

After Nick Wang led off the bottom of the eighth with a fly out to center field on the first pitch of his at-bat, Toomey finally broke the 2–2 stalemate. He sent a line drive home run into the corner in right field, giving BC its first lead of the day. That was all the offense the Eagles could muster, but it was enough to carry them into the final frame up 3–2. 

That didn’t last long, though. 

Liberty led off with back-to-back base hits to put two runners in scoring position. An RBI groundout from Jordan Jaffe tied the game 3–3 and placed Riley DeCandido on third base, threatening a go-ahead run. BC looked like it would tag DeCandido out on the following play, as Solier sent the ball to catcher Gunnar Johnson on a fielder’s choice. But the throw flew right of Johnson’s head and DeCandido got home safely, giving Liberty a one-run lead. 

As they had all game, the Eagles struggled offensively in the ninth. Johnson was hit by a pitch, then pinch runner Esteban Garcia advanced to second on a sacrifice groundout from Ben Williams. But back-to-back fly outs from Hendrickson and Solier ended the frame, and the Eagles lost their NCAA Tournament opener. 

“It’s a really good game,” Interdonato said. “It just hurts being on this end of it.”

The post BC Falls to Liberty 4–3 After Ninth-Inning Collapse in Opening Game of NCAA Tournament appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: Trump Announces He Will Posthumously Award Welles Crowther Presidential Medal of Freedom
news_bc.author: Nikita Osadchiy
news_bc.summary:
President Donald Trump announced he would posthumously award Welles Crowther, BC ’99, with the Presidential Medal of Freedom—one of the nation’s highest civilian honors—during a campaign rally in New York Friday evening.

“We are approaching the 25th anniversary of September 11, 2001, a dark day that will live in infamy,” Trump said. “We are posthumously awarding Welles the Presidential Medal of Freedom—which is the highest award outside of the Congressional Medal of Honor.”

Trump’s announcement came several months after Congressman Mike Lawler (R-N.Y.) publicly urged the president to recognize Crowther’s heroism.

The president traveled to Rockland County, N.Y.—Crowther’s hometown—to campaign for Lawler in his competitive district.

Crowther was a volunteer firefighter. On the morning of Sept. 11, 2001, Crowther was working as an equities trader in the South Tower of the World Trade Center. 

After two hijacked passenger planes struck the Twin Towers, he led a group of survivors from the 78th floor down to safety, helping save as many as 18 lives. 

He chose to rush back into the burning tower in an effort to rescue more people. When the tower collapsed, Crowther was killed. He was 24. 

Survivors later identified Crowther as “the man in the red bandanna,” a reference to the distinctive handkerchief he wore on that day while helping others escape.

The president congratulated Welles’ mother, Alison Crowther, who stood onstage beside him wearing Welles’ signature red bandanna around her neck.

“I just want to congratulate his great mother in doing a phenomenal job in raising that young man,” Trump said. “Boy, what bravery saved those people, and [he] became a legend. No one else would have done what he did.”

Alison Crowther thanked the president before addressing the crowd with her own remarks.

“To me, it is such a beautiful thing that even 25 years later, Welles’ light still shines brightly,” she said. “I travel around the country—and I’ve even been to Amman, Jordan—talking about Welles’ story, and his legacy and his example of courage, fearlessness … and his pure humanity that day saving so many lives and as I travel and speak to children, they’re tremendously moved and inspired by this to be better people.”

Alison Crowther also thanked The New York Times for uncovering Welles’ story.

Welles Crowther’s legacy remains deeply embedded in the Boston College community. Every fall, the University honors him with the annual Red Bandanna Game, commemorating his sacrifice in saving the lives of others during the attack.

His name is also engraved on the 9/11 Memorial Labyrinth outside Burns Library, on BC’s campus, alongside 21 other alumni who lost their lives that day.

During his announcement, Trump did not state when the award ceremony would take place.

The post Trump Announces He Will Posthumously Award Welles Crowther Presidential Medal of Freedom appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: BC’s Offense Falls Flat, Miami Beats Eagles 8–2 In ACC Tournament
news_bc.author: Maria Stefanoudakis
news_bc.summary:
Boston College baseball head coach Todd Interdonato lights up when asked about senior pitcher A.J. Colarusso. He is reliable. Hard-working. Consistent. Best of all, he almost always gives the Eagles a chance to win. 

In No. 4-seed BC’s ACC Quarterfinal matchup against No. 5-seed Miami, however, Colarusso didn’t quite possess his normal swag. He gave up six runs, tying his season-high in earned runs on a season-high 10 hits in five innings pitched. 

Without Colarusso’s steadying presence and lacking any offensive momentum, the Eagles (36–21, 17–13 Atlantic Coast) fell 8–2 to Miami (38–17, 16–14) on Thursday afternoon, and were knocked out of the conference tournament hosted in Charlotte, N.C. 

Before their ultimate collapse, the Eagles shot out to a quick lead. Colarusso shut Miami down in the top of the first, as the Hurricanes stranded two on base. Then, following a few fruitless offensive outings last weekend, BC’s offense looked more like itself in the bottom half of the opening frame. 

“I thought our worst offensive game in the last handful of weeks was game one against Georgia Tech, and then I feel like we’ve had this slow climb out of it,” Interdonato said. 

Ty Mainolfi drew a walk, then advanced to second on a throwing error by Miami starter Lazaro Collera. Then, back-to-back RBI base hits from Jack Toomey and Kyle Wolff put two BC runs on the board. Wolff’s RBI double scored the Eagles’ last run of the day, though. Their offense quickly turned stale, as BC managed just three hits over the final eight innings.  

“In order for us to play at our best, we need three, four, five good plate appearances in a row,” Interdonato said. “One swing, one home run, one bases-clearing double—that’s not who we are.” 

Meanwhile, Miami’s bats heated up. Colarusso struggled in the second, and a four-run inning from the Hurricanes extinguished the Eagles’ lead. Miami racked up five hits in the frame, including a two-RBI home run, and Jake Ogden scored on a wild pitch to make it 4–2.

“When this game started, our whole objective was to get in control of the game—put up the first zero, score the first run—which is what we did,” Interdonato said. “But then they answered with that four-spot in the second that gave them control of it back.” 

Colarusso found more success in the third, and Julio Solier opened the bottom of the inning with a single, giving the Eagles a chance to recover their spark. But Collera retired the next three in order to end the inning, including striking out Nick Wang, BC’s top batter. 

“They were in control of the at-bats the whole way,” Interdonato said. 

Another two-RBI home run in the top of the fourth pushed Miami’s lead to four, as Alex Sosa’s shot arched over the net in left-center and dropped into the dark green seats in Truist Field. Luke Gallo attempted to steal a base in the bottom of the frame—a tactic that’s come in handy for BC all season—but was caught. Nothing, even the things they normally draw success from, was going right for the Eagles. 

After giving up a single and a hit-by-pitch in the fifth, Interdonato came out to speak with Colarusso. Colarusso stayed in and didn’t allow any additional runs, but Kyle Kipp relieved him to start the sixth. 

Last year, Colarusso might have pushed through, stayed in the game, and tried to figure things out. But this year is unlike last in a key way: The Eagles are expected to play in an NCAA Regional. With the postseason looming, BC will almost certainly need Colarusso rested and ready. 

Thursday’s game was not the end-all, be-all it would have been at this time last year. And while Colarusso wasn’t able to give the Eagles the spark he provided in last year’s ACC Tournament run, he’s been a cornerstone of BC’s success this season. 

“I’m grateful we competed as hard as we did in the regular season to give us this opportunity, and I am starting looking forward to next week,” Colarusso said. “We’re just gonna put our best foot forward, tomorrow and this whole week, and get ready to make some noise in a regional.”

Now finished with ACC action, the Eagles have their sights set on a much bigger goal: making it to Omaha and earning the right to play for a national title.  

“There’s a lot of anger in that dugout right now—there’s a lot of angst in that dugout,” Interdonato said. “I know you can look at the totality of this year and feel really good about it, but you’ve got 40 competitors in the dugout, kind of getting it taken to them. Our guys in there are pretty pissed off. I don’t think I’m gonna have to motivate them in practice this week.”

The post BC’s Offense Falls Flat, Miami Beats Eagles 8–2 In ACC Tournament appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: Boston College adds pair of special teamers to 2027 class
news_bc.author: Greg Patuto
news_bc.summary:
A duo of special team players out of Florida decided to stick together and come North.

On Friday, kicker Mateo Sanchez and long snapper Aidan Field announced commitments to Boston College. The duo, both from North Broward Prep in Coconut Creek, Florida, visited campus at different times earlier this month before committing.

Sanchez held offers from the likes of Florida State, Duke, and Army. He attended camps at Notre Dame and Georgia Tech as well.

“Thank you to all the coaches who have been with me along this journey, thank you to my parents who have been by my side through all the highs and lows. GO EAGLES!,” Sanchez wrote.

The two sent out posts on the same morning. Field was a Rubio Top 12 invitee this year. The 6-foot-4, 205-pound snapper has a snap time of .68 seconds.

“Thank you to the Boston College coaching staff for this incredible opportunity. I am so grateful to everyone who has supported me along the way, especially my coaches, parents, family, teammates, and friends. I wouldn’t be here without your constant support, encouragement, and belief in me,” Field wrote.

The Eagles now have a total of 29 commits in the Class of 2027. Bill O’Brien has been hitting all aspects of the game in recruiting recently. How do you feel about Boston College’s class next year?

news_bc.title: Boston College Football: Secondary Preview
news_bc.author: Joseph Celia
news_bc.summary:
A pivotal season awaits Boston College Football. The league year is vastly approaching, and it’s going to look a lot different versus last year’s team — to be determined for better or for worse. In regards to 2025-26, one area the team must continue to build on is their secondary. 

Last season, the Eagles allowed the eighth most passing yards in the ACC out of 17 teams. In what was nothing short of a disappointing year, the secondary was one of their few consistent and steady areas on the team. With it being arguably their strongest positional group, they’re retaining a well-established and experienced core that should only improve once the season begins. BC is also bringing in a new DC in Ted Roof, whose defensive schemes rely heavily on zone coverage — another element to watch for. 

The best players in the Eagles secondary group are safeties KP Price and Carter Davis. Both are highly graded in their positional groups. Price returns after leading the team with 93 tackles in 2025 and 2 INT’s. Davis had 59 tackles total and 1 sack. Their cornerbacks group include Max Tucker, Syair Torrence, Isaiah Farris, and Ashton Cunningham. Tucker is only one in this CB group with an INT from last season, but a new defensive scheme can help change that. As for the NB’s, this group includes Cameron Martinez and TJ Green. Roof’s system will benefit them tremendously, as they’ll have a more clear vision of the field to make more plays. 

It’s easy to point to the quarterback and coach as being the keys in achieving in 2026-27 for BC Football. But for the sake of the argument, the team’s secondary could make or break the defense this year, and something that they must continue to build on.

news_bc.title: BC Football Summer Preview: Offensive Line
news_bc.author: Niraj Patel
news_bc.summary:
For the first two seasons of the Bill O’Brien era, the Boston College offensive line was a marriage between old and new: Hafley‑recruited personnel, a holdover position coach, and just enough continuity to keep the Eagles from fully tearing it down up front. Now, Matt Applebaum has moved on and with Kurt Anderson having taken over the room, 2026 is no longer an inheritance and instead in the name, image, and likeness, if you will, of Bill O’Brien.

The Eagles effectively lose four starters on the offensive line, three to the NFL in Bowry (4th Rd), Taylor (6th Rd), Cline (UDFA) and one to transfer (Daugherty), along with a few other rotational guys. Although the unit did struggle in most facets, this is a lot of experience to replace. That means we have a lot of open questions coming into the season and heavy competition.

New offensive coach Kurt Anderson comes in well-regarded from previous coaching spots most notably at Northwestern. He quickly attacked the portal and brought in six transfer lineman to account for only having one starter returning, center Michael Crounse, and one other returner who logged a start in Robert Smith IV.

Starting in the interior, Crounse (R-Jr) anchored the last eight games of the season. He will look to continue his development and should provide a stable base. Yonkers native DJ Williams played in a game last year, maintaining his redshirt, could be the backup here. Smith ended up starting five games across both guard spots and as a true freshman no less. He’s going to stay in the mix regardless of who has been brought in, and his versatility will help him continue to log snaps. Judah Pruitt is the other main returner who spent last year backing up at guard. He got his feet wet in most games, primarily on the kicking units, but could move more into a rotational role. He’s seen some time this offseason at center too.

Kristian Phillips is the first of our transfers. A senior from Michigan State, he was a solid prospect out of high school and flashed that ceiling collegiately, but had his path was knocked off course by injuries. He still managed four starts at right guard last year, but this season feels like a new start for him in a new room and staring at a depth chart that should give him the inside track on locking down a starting role. Owen Snively’s a super-senior who has made 16 career starts between Eastern Michigan and Colorado State (with Daz). The veteran experience will be apt for a plug and play guy, well-suited at left guard where he has more experience, but could flip as needed. I expect the last starting spot to come down to him or Robert Smith, but both should see plenty of work this season at the very least as the extra lineman coming in. Jani Norwood comes to us via the other Bill at UNC. He adds another layer of upside with a hint of experience as a redshirt sophomore. He appeared in six games and started a bowl game as a freshman.

On the tackle front, we have a couple of intriguing prospects. Starting with key returners, redshirt sophomore Pape Abdoulaye Sy returns after serving as Jude Bowry’s backup at left tackle. The 6-7 NFL Academy prospect came in for a bit against Notre Dame and SMU as well.

Trevon Humphrey is jumping up a level from NC Central, but arrives with a heavy workload of 36 starts under his belt in a high scoring offense. He’s a 6-5 upperclassman as is Reggie Jackson from Jacksonville State. Jackson has a strong athletic profile and made 11 starts at right tackle on a top rushing offense. He’s seen some cross-training at guard. Veguer Jean-Jumeau is a redshirt sophomore from Tennessee State. At 6’9 tackle, he was recruited heavily by some other big programs like Miss State and Vandy, so he might have seen BC as the place where he was most likely to get early minutes with a good track record. My guess is the starters will be two of Jackson, Sy, or Humphrey at first, but fully expect to see a few combinations. Given the style of offense under Mason McKenzie, I could see Jackson’s profile giving him the edge.

Amir Johnson is a name to watch among returning players. He transferred in from Merrimack last year where he played left tackle. He was mostly on special teams last year, but could crack the depth chart after a year in the program as a redshirt junior. Souleye Diawara is young, but has a huge frame at 6-9, 340. He was on the special teams unit and played in ten games. The remainder of the crop are freshman we outlined here, but the main callouts would have to be local four-star product Marc Antunes Jr. and Bruno Werner from the NFL Academy.

It might take a bit for this group to really gel with all the new faces and the new coach, and I have a sneaky feeling we’re in for a season where we won’t see the same combination more than a handful of times. There’s a lot of potential here and seemingly good depth, so it’s more a credit to that than a doomsday theory. Still, this feels like the first deliberate build rather than the holdover patch job, albeit with some pretty dang good players.

news_bc.title: Boston College loses major weapon for 2026 season
news_bc.author: Greg Patuto
news_bc.summary:
Boston College will be without one of its biggest offensive weapons for the upcoming season.

On Thursday morning, head coach Bill O’Brien announced that tight end Kaelan Chudzinski will miss the 2026 campaign with an Achilles injury.

Chudzinski was slated to be the team’s starting tight end this season after emerging as one of the nation’s best freshmen at the position in 2025. He totaled 24 catches for 313 yards and four touchdowns in his first season.

With Jeremiah Franklin moving on, this set the stage for Chudzinski to take over as TE1 this season and for the future.

O’Brien shared that he expects Chudzinski to return to football activities in January 2027.

news_bc.title: Boston College Football among toughest schedules in the nation
news_bc.author: Greg Patuto
news_bc.summary:
The Boston College football schedule has been set for months now. Right away, it was clear that the Eagles will be challenged each week.

Boston College is coming off a 2-10 campaign in 2025. The team is looking to improve and to do so, it will have to pick up some hard-fought victories.

Last week, ESPN shared its latest Football Power Index, including strength of schedule of all teams around the nation. In this list, Boston College currently has the 24th hardest schedule in the country this fall.

Boston College Football Schedule

  • Sept. 5: at Cincinnati
  • Sept. 11: vs. Rutgers
  • Sept. 19: vs. Maine
  • Sept. 26: vs. Virginia Tech
  • Oct. 3: at SMU
  • Oct. 17: vs. Pitt
  • Oct. 24: at Georgia Tech
  • Oct. 31: at Duke
  • Nov. 7: vs. Florida State
  • Nov. 14: at Notre Dame
  • Nov. 21: vs. Syracuse
  • Nov. 28: at Miami

The Good

There is a chance that Boston College reaches its win total from last season in Week 3, or maybe even Week 2 if all goes right. For the ultimate optimist out there, is it possible the Eagles double their win total from 2025 by Week 4?

Obviously, no games outside of Maine can be penciled in as a victory. The season opens against Cincinnati before the home opener against Rutgers and a date with Virginia Tech in Week 4. These are three winnable games for the Eagles. In total, Boston College will play five teams that did not play in bowl season last year.

That is the absolute best-case scenario. In reality, Boston College will be a touchdown underdog at least against Cincinnati. The Rutgers game is a massive swing on the schedule before Maine. The minimum goal for the Eagles in the first four should be a split.

The Bad

After those first four games of the season, it does not get easier as Boston College hits the road for three out of four.

A trip to SMU begins October before back-to-back against Georgia Tech and Duke on the road. A home game with Pitt is mixed in. This game is coming off a bye week. The Eagles will have two weeks to prepare for a home game against the Panthers. This is another swing game on the schedule.

Boston College will not play Cal, Clemson, Louisville, North Carolina, NC State, Stanford, Virginia, Wake Forest this year. The Eagles avoid trips out West but Cal and Stanford would have been welcomed. This means the ugly is coming in the ACC.

The Ugly

The final four games of the season will be a gauntlet for the Eagles.

Florida State begins the final third of the year. The Seminoles will come North to play this game but it will be a difficult one for the Eagles. The Eagles will also have a trip to Notre Dame scheduled. The Irish are one of the favorites to win the National Championship this season. Playing this game on the road makes for a massive spread.

Boston College ends the season with a road game against Miami, who finished as the national runner-up last season.

news_bc.title: 5 questions surrounding Boston College entering 2026 ACC Media Days
news_bc.author: Greg Patuto
news_bc.summary:
The ACC is up next on the media days calendar.

The conference will showcase its teams beginning on Wednesday from the Hilton Uptown in Charlotte. Bill O’Brien and Boston College will take the podium on Thursday and there are many burning questions surrounding the Eagles.

Boston College is looking to improve off a 2-10 season in 2025. There are question marks on both sides of the ball on the field along with others off the field to ponder during media days.

1. Can a new offensive line gel right away?

The offensive line struggled in both passing and running situations last season. Even though there were not many losses in the transfer portal, the Eagles knew that they needed to make additions to improve the offensive line.

Boston College brought in guard Kristian Phillips from Michigan State along with tackles Reggie Jackson from Jacksonville State and North Carolina Central’s Trevon Humphrey. It is likely that both Phillips and Jackson will land starting spots along the offensive line. With two new starters and an entire unit that needs to improve, it will be interesting to see how the Eagles perform up front.

2. Mason McKenzie: Will it work?

It was a battle under center for the Eagles but it looks as though the transfer from Saginaw Valley State will come into the season as QB1.

Mason McKenzie committed to Boston College in January and was a contender to start despite his lack of Division I experience, along with Grayson Willson. The book on McKenzie is that he comes to the Eagles with a strong arm and an ability to use his legs. At just 6-foot-1, McKenzie will have to be able to move in the pocket. This fits into what Bill O’Brien likes to do.

The question is — can his game translate to the Division I level and find success right away?

3. Will there be a run game?

The Eagles ranked 15th in the ACC last season with 103.9 rushing yards per game. This was partially because of a disastrous offensive line, but it should be no surprise that the coaching staff went to bring in some new blood. Liberty standout Evan Dickens rushed for 1,339 yards and 16 touchdowns last season. He will now lead the backfield for Boston College, giving the offense an intriguing duo of runners with McKenzie in the mix. The Eagles also loaded up with Nolan Ray transferring in from Maryland. This will be an important part of the offense in order to keep defenses on their toes.

4. Can the defense take the ball away?

This one is self explanatory. The Boston College defense ranked 128th in total last season. Ted Roof came in to be the defensive coordinator and has his hands full but turnovers must be the focal point. Boston College had just six interceptions and six fumbles recovered last season. The team forced 13 fewer turnovers than it did in 2024. The pass rush needs to get better but an experience group in the secondary returns led by KP Price and Carter Davis. The Eagles must find a way to generate more turnovers.

5. Will anyone get to three sacks?

This sounds like a simple feat, right? Wrong.

Boston College was last in the ACC with 17 sacks last season. Sedarius McConnell led the team with 2.5 sacks. That means no Eagle reached the three-sack mark in 12 games played.

The Eagles hit the portal along the defensive line as well in hopes of improving these numbers. Georgia transfer Kris Jones will get the chance to showcase himself after one year in Athens. Harvard transfer Alex DeGrieck and Buffalo transfer Demetrius Ballard also have big-time upside.

news_bc.title: Where does Bill O’Brien rank among ACC head coaches?
news_bc.author: Greg Patuto
news_bc.summary:
Bill O’Brien is entering his third year as head coach of Boston College and his first two could not have been more different.

O’Brien was hired in 2024 and went 7-5 in his first season with a trip to the Pinstripe Bowl, which was a 20-15 loss to Nebraska. O’Brien and the Eagles finished 2-10 in 2025.

O’Brien has been around college football and the NFL for a long time. He spent six full years with the Houston Texans before being fired in 2020 following an 0-4 start. He then bounced around as offensive coordinator at Alabama, Ohio State, and with the New England Patriots.

O’Brien has now settled into his new home at Boston College looking to improve upon what was a disastrous 2025 campaign.

The itch for college football has arrived in full force. This means countless previews, predictions, and rankings about anything and everything. This includes a ranking of ACC head coaches made by Austin Curtright of USA Today.

The question is, where does Bill O’Brien rank? The publication ranked O’Brien 16th out of 17 coaches:

“O’Brien went 7-6 in his first season at Boston College, but failed to build momentum with a terrible 2-10 finish in 2025. After a successful start to his tenure with the Houston Texans, winning four AFC South titles from 2014-2019, he was fired after an 0-4 start in 2020. O’Brien then spent multiple seasons as the offensive coordinator at Alabama and then the New England Patriots in 2023 and Ohio State in 2024, winning a national title with the program. O’Brien went 15-9 in two seasons as Penn State’s head coach from 2012-13 before bouncing for the NFL, but his college head coaching career largely hasn’t been all that impressive.” — Austin Curtright, USA Today

The position on the list has to do with a short tenure paired with a two-win season. O’Brien has a chance to improve his standing next year if the Eagles can win a few more games this fall.

Check out the full list below:

  1. Mario Cristobal, Miami
  2. Dabo Swinney, Clemson
  3. James Franklin, Virginia Tech
  4. Rhett Lashlee, SMU
  5. Jeff Brohm, Louisville
  6. Brent Key, Georgia Tech
  7. Manny Diaz, Duke
  8. Jake Dickert, Wake Forest
  9. Pat Narduzzi, Pitt
  10. Tony Elliott, Virginia
  11. Dave Doeren, NC State
  12. Fran Brown, Syracuse
  13. Mike Norvell, Florida State
  14. Tosh Lupoi, Cal
  15. Tavita Pritchard, Stanford
  16. Bill O’Brien, Boston College
  17. Bill Belichick, North Carolina

Do you agree with these rankings?

news_bc.title: Boston College wraps up weekend with new commit
news_bc.author: Greg Patuto
news_bc.summary:
After Boston College landed its second commit in the 2028 class in Mikey Landers, the coaching staff remained busy and added more pieces.

On Thursday, three-star tight end Chase Phifer announced his commitment to Boston College. He becomes the 27th commit in the 2027 class.

““100% committed,” Phifer wrote on X. “Extremely blessed and excited to announce my commitment to Boston College!! Thank you God for allowing me to play this game!”

The 6-foot-5 tight end chose the Eagles over the likes of Wake Forest, UCF, Memphis, Coastal Carolina, UComm, Toledo, Tulane, and more.

Phifer received his offer from head coach Bill O’Brien and the Eagles two weeks ago and quickly took a visit to campus. After meeting with the staff and touring the facilities, Phifer decided to commit.

The Eagles have been busy in recent weeks. Prior to Landers announcing his commitment, Boston College was hard at work in the 2027 class. This includes landing three-star defensive and offensive lineman Krystian Walcott.

The 6-foot-4 product of Buford High School in Georgia originally committed to Miami (Ohio). He held many offers from programs like Auburn, Florida State, Mississippi State, Nebraska, Texas, Ole Miss, Tennessee, and Oregon. He flipped to Boston College, giving the Eagles a highly touted prospect in the trenches.

He was an assistant coach at York University for one season, 2012-13. Since then he has mainly been...

news_college_hockey.title: BC Signs First WHL Player
news_college_hockey.summary: Boston College has not dipped its toes that far into the Major Junior ranks yet, but after bringing in two OHL players in the last couple years, the Eagles have committed their first player from the WHL.Matias Vanhanen, who scored 87 points in 62 games for Everett last season, is expected to join BC this coming season. Vanhanen, a native of Nokia, Finland, was selected in the second round, 37th overall...

news_college_hockey.title: Michigan AD Manuel Agrees to Buyout

But it finally became clear that a change was the best course of action. Manuel will remain on the job through the end of the year, while consulting on his replacement.Michigan's athletic...

news_college_hockey.title: New Government Rule Jeopardizes 5th-Year Eligibility
news_college_hockey.summary: Less than a month after the NCAA approved new age rules that included five years of eligibility for all players, a United States government rule change could eliminate the opportunity for half of college hockey.The pros and cons of the NCAA's new eligibility standards have been well-debated for months. news_ktn.title: Secret Service agent on Vance’s detail suspected of leaking information is put on leave amid internal review
news_ktn.author: bounce-cn2-ZH_CNNAlerts_NDBAN07232026c155001-h-3c362c1dcd=2@response.emailalerts.cnn.com (bounce-cn2-ZH_CNNAlerts_NDBAN07232026c155001-h-3c362c1dcd=2@response.emailalerts.cnn.com)
news_ktn.summary:
Get the latest updates from CNN
 

Thursday, July 23, 2026

View in browser 
 

Secret Service agent on Vance’s detail suspected of leaking information is put on leave amid internal review

A Secret Service agent on Vice President JD Vance's security detail is under internal review and has been put on administrative leave after being suspected of leaking information for a news story that included details about Vance’s travel, a source familiar with the matter said.

Read more

news_ktn.summary:

Wednesday, July 22, 2026

House takes first step to unlock Iran war funding — but fate in Senate uncertain, with measure tied to ‘SAVE America Act’

The House of Representatives on Wednesday took the first step toward funding President Donald Trump’s war with Iran, as well as approving tens of billions in additional Pentagon money and aid for US farmers.

news_ktn.author: msprvs1=206633RHqIeV7=bounces-280172-2012@bounces.washingtonpost.com (msprvs1=206633RHqIeV7=bounces-280172-2012@bounces.washingtonpost.com)
news_ktn.summary:
The agreement with a kingdom signaling it wants a nuclear weapon has sparked alarm among nonproliferation experts. Congress has 90 days to act on the deal.

news_ktn.author: bounce-cn2-ZH_CNNAlerts_NDBAN07222026c155001-h-a16e86e771=2@response.emailalerts.cnn.com (bounce-cn2-ZH_CNNAlerts_NDBAN07222026c155001-h-a16e86e771=2@response.emailalerts.cnn.com)
news_ktn.summary:
Get the latest updates from CNN
 

Wednesday, July 22, 2026

View in browser 
 

US signs deal with Saudi Arabia that could allow kingdom to enrich nuclear fuel

President Donald Trump has approved a nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia that could lead to the kingdom enriching its own fuel for civilian reactors without adopting the “gold standard” of international oversight, according to two US officials and a source familiar with the matter.

news_ktn.title: Speaking truth to power
news_ktn.author: bounce-525PWGB4WGFUPANCRMQCVSIIPE.60269@bounce.nl.npr.org (bounce-525PWGB4WGFUPANCRMQCVSIIPE.60269@bounce.nl.npr.org)
news_ktn.summary:
We report stories that keep you informed without favor or bias.
View this email online
&&&&&&&&&&&&&&
Shannon Bond
Correspondent, NPR
The world never slows down — and neither do I or my fellow journalists. 

Last month, I reported on how groups tied to the artificial intelligence and tech industries are trying to influence the midterm elections to shape the future of AI regulation. I dove deep into how money is flooding into races across the country and who is seeking to benefit. 

I report stories like these knowing that our journalism is independent and not influenced by special interests. No matter who we’re reporting about, the NPR Network reports the facts without favor or bias.

Your support allows us to tell these stories, no matter who the subject is. Together, we continue the kind of journalism that keeps you informed and holds the powerful to account. 

Don’t wait – with just a few days left in July, we need to close this month strong. Join the community of people who power this work by making a donation today. As we transform to thrive without federal funding, your gift ensures the NPR Network can continue delivering journalism that serves you — and only you — the public.
Donate Now ➔
For the people, by the people,

Shannon Bond

PS. Public media is sustained by the public it serves. Thank you!

ICE officials warned of dangerous overcrowding at a N.Y. facility: ‘This is insane.’ People kept getting packed in.
